\# Results

\#\# Experimental Setup

All runs used the following configuration:

| Parameter | Value |  
|-----------|-------|  
| Task | Distinguish between *\*sad\**, *\*angry\**, and *\*happy\** human faces |  
| Prompt | "Finetune my model to distinguish between sad, angry, and happy human faces." |  
| Epochs | 3 |  
| Learning Rate | 0.0001 |

Eight experimental runs were conducted, varying the backbone architecture and data augmentation strategy. Each run reports baseline accuracy (ImageNet-pretrained head, no fine-tuning) and fine-tuned accuracy on the test set.

\---

\#\# Summary of Results  
![][image1]

| Run | Configuration | Baseline Acc. (%) | Fine-tuned Acc. (%) | Gain (pp) |  
|-----|---------------|:-----------------:|:-------------------:|:---------:|  
| 1 | ResNet\-50 | 30.29 | 62.86 | \+32.57 |  
| 2 | MobileNet\-V3 | 29.14 | **\*\*71.43\*\*** | \+42.29 |  
| 3 | EfficientNet\-B0 | 30.86 | 68.00 | \+37.14 |  
| 4 | ConvNeXt\-Tiny | **\*\*38.29\*\*** | 68.57 | \+30.28 |  
| 5 | ResNet\-50 \+ Data Augmentation | 35.37 | 67.35 | \+31.98 |  
| 6 | ResNet\-50 \+ Diffusion Augmentation | 29.93 | 65.31 | \+35.38 |  
| 7 | ResNet\-50 \+ CLIP Filtering | 37.29 | 62.86 | \+25.57 |  
| 8 | ResNet\-50 \+ All Features | 26.40 | 60.43 | \+34.03 |

Fine-tuning consistently improved accuracy across all runs, with gains ranging from **\*\*+25.6 to \+42.3 percentage points\*\***.

\---

\#\# Per-Run Results

\#\#\# Run 1 — ResNet-50 (Baseline Architecture)

\- **\*\*Baseline accuracy:\*\*** 30.29%  
\- **\*\*Fine-tuned accuracy:\*\*** 62.86%

*\*Per-class accuracy chart — Baseline vs. Fine-tuned (ResNet-50)\**

\> The model completely failed to detect the *\*sad\** class before fine-tuning (0.0%), but recovered to 42.2% after. The *\*angry\** and *\*happy\** classes showed more modest gains (65.4% → 63.5% and 32.2% → 84.7%, respectively).

\---

\#\#\# Run 2 — MobileNet-V3

\- **\*\*Baseline accuracy:\*\*** 29.14%  
\- **\*\*Fine-tuned accuracy:\*\*** 71.43%  *\*(highest among all runs)\**

*\*Per-class accuracy chart — Baseline vs. Fine-tuned (MobileNet-V3)\**

\> MobileNet-V3 achieved the best overall fine-tuned accuracy, with strong and balanced per-class performance across all three emotion categories after fine-tuning.

\---

\#\#\# Run 3 — EfficientNet-B0

\- **\*\*Baseline accuracy:\*\*** 30.86%  
\- **\*\*Fine-tuned accuracy:\*\*** 68.00%

*\*Per-class accuracy chart — Baseline vs. Fine-tuned (EfficientNet-B0)\**

\> EfficientNet-B0 showed substantial gains across all three classes, with a notably low baseline on *\*angry\** (13.5%) that improved to 65.4% after fine-tuning.

\---

\#\#\# Run 4 — ConvNeXt-Tiny

\- **\*\*Baseline accuracy:\*\*** 38.29%  *\*(highest baseline among all runs)\**  
\- **\*\*Fine-tuned accuracy:\*\*** 68.57%

*\*Per-class accuracy chart — Baseline vs. Fine-tuned (ConvNeXt-Tiny)\**

\> ConvNeXt-Tiny had the strongest out-of-the-box performance, reflecting its modern architectural priors. However, despite the head start, it did not surpass MobileNet-V3 after fine-tuning.

\---

\#\#\# Run 5 — ResNet-50 with Standard Data Augmentation

\- **\*\*Baseline accuracy:\*\*** 35.37%  
\- **\*\*Fine-tuned accuracy:\*\*** 67.35%

*\*Per-class accuracy chart — Baseline vs. Fine-tuned (ResNet-50 \+ Augmentation)\**

\> Standard data augmentation improved the ResNet-50 baseline by \~5 percentage points and lifted fine-tuned accuracy by \~4.5pp over the plain ResNet-50. Notably, the *\*happy\** class baseline was 0.0%, suggesting augmentation shifted the class distribution.

\---

\#\#\# Run 6 — ResNet-50 with Diffusion-Generated Image Augmentation

\- **\*\*Baseline accuracy:\*\*** 29.93%  
\- **\*\*Fine-tuned accuracy:\*\*** 65.31%

*\*Per-class accuracy chart — Baseline vs. Fine-tuned (ResNet-50 \+ Diffusion)\**

\> Diffusion-generated augmentation produced a middle-ground result — outperforming the plain ResNet-50 fine-tuned accuracy (62.86%) but falling short of standard augmentation (67.35%), suggesting that synthetic images provide useful diversity but may introduce distributional noise.

\---

\#\#\# Run 7 — ResNet-50 with CLIP-Based Filtering

\- **\*\*Baseline accuracy:\*\*** 37.29%  
\- **\*\*Fine-tuned accuracy:\*\*** 62.86%

*\*Per-class accuracy chart — Baseline vs. Fine-tuned (ResNet-50 \+ CLIP)\**

\> CLIP filtering produced the second-highest baseline (37.29%), indicating that quality-filtered data improves zero-shot representations. However, this advantage did not transfer to fine-tuning — the fine-tuned accuracy matched the plain ResNet-50 (62.86%), the lowest among augmentation strategies. The *\*sad\** class saw a significant regression (83.7% → 40.8%) post fine-tuning, warranting further investigation.

\---

\#\#\# Run 8 — ResNet-50 with All Features Enabled

\- **\*\*Baseline accuracy:\*\*** 26.40%  *\*(lowest baseline among all runs)\**  
\- **\*\*Fine-tuned accuracy:\*\*** 60.43%  *\*(lowest among ResNet-50 variants)\**

*\*Per-class accuracy chart — Baseline vs. Fine-tuned (ResNet-50 \+ All Features)\**

\> Combining all augmentation strategies simultaneously produced the worst performance among ResNet-50 variants in both baseline and fine-tuned accuracy. This indicates that naively stacking augmentation pipelines can interfere with training stability and introduce conflicting noise signals that hurt convergence.

\---

\#\# Analysis and Discussion

\#\#\# Architecture Comparison (Runs 1–4)

Among the four backbone architectures tested without augmentation, **\*\*MobileNet-V3 achieved the highest fine-tuned accuracy (71.43%)\*\***, outperforming ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0, and ConvNeXt-Tiny. This suggests that lighter, depthwise-separable architectures generalize better to this facial emotion dataset, potentially because their inductive biases align well with local texture features relevant to emotion recognition.

ConvNeXt-Tiny stands out for having the best baseline (38.29%), reflecting stronger ImageNet-pretrained feature representations. Yet it did not surpass MobileNet-V3 after fine-tuning, implying that baseline feature quality and fine-tuning performance are not perfectly correlated for this task.

\#\#\# Effect of Augmentation Strategies on ResNet-50 (Runs 1, 5–8)

| Strategy | Fine-tuned Acc. (%) | vs. Plain ResNet-50 |  
|----------|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|  
| None (Run 1\) | 62.86 | — |  
| Data Augmentation (Run 5\) | 67.35 | \+4.49 |  
| Diffusion Augmentation (Run 6\) | 65.31 | \+2.45 |  
| CLIP Filtering (Run 7\) | 62.86 | \+0.00 |  
| All Combined (Run 8\) | 60.43 | −2.43 |

Standard data augmentation offered the most consistent benefit. Diffusion-generated images provided a modest gain, while CLIP filtering raised the baseline but did not improve fine-tuned accuracy. Combining all strategies hurt performance, suggesting diminishing or negative returns from stacking augmentation methods without careful tuning.

\#\#\# Key Takeaways

1\. **\*\*Fine-tuning is highly effective:\*\*** All architectures showed 22–42 pp gains over baseline, confirming that task-specific fine-tuning is essential for facial emotion recognition.  
2\. **\*\*Lighter architectures can outperform heavier ones:\*\*** MobileNet-V3 surpassed ResNet-50 and ConvNeXt-Tiny after fine-tuning despite having fewer parameters.  
3\. **\*\*Strong baselines do not guarantee strong fine-tuning outcomes:\*\*** ConvNeXt-Tiny and CLIP-filtered ResNet-50 had the best baselines but did not achieve the best fine-tuned accuracy.  
4\. **\*\*Augmentation stacking requires care:\*\*** Naively combining data augmentation, diffusion generation, and CLIP filtering degraded both baseline and fine-tuned accuracy (Run 8), highlighting the need for principled augmentation selection.

[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>
